concept.title: What Is DITA?
concept.shortdesc: DITA (Darwin Information Typing Architecture) is an XML-based open standard for authoring modular, reusable technical content that can be published to multiple formats from a single source.

section.title: What is the Darwin Information Typing Architecture?
section.p: DITA (Darwin Information Typing Architecture) is an XML-based standard for authoring, producing, and delivering technical information. Originally developed at IBM in the early 2000s, it is now an OASIS open standard used by organizations worldwide — from enterprise software companies to medical device manufacturers.

DITA organizes content into typed topics — concepts, tasks, and references — that are assembled into deliverables using maps. This separation of content from presentation enables multi-channel publishing and intelligent content delivery.

section.title: What are the core concepts in DITA?
section.p: DITA is built on three foundational ideas:

ul.li: Topics: Self-contained units of information, each covering a single subject. A topic can stand on its own or be assembled with other topics into a larger deliverable.

Maps: Organizational files that define how topics are arranged, sequenced, and related. Maps control the table of contents, navigation hierarchy, and cross-references — without modifying the topics themselves.

Specialization: A mechanism for extending the DITA vocabulary to meet domain-specific needs while preserving compatibility with the base standard. Specialized elements inherit processing behavior from their ancestors.

section.title: What are the standard DITA topic types?
section.p: DITA defines three base information types, each with a distinct structure optimized for its purpose:

ul.li: Concept (<concept>) — Explains what something is and why it matters. Contains sections with explanatory text, lists, and examples.
Task (<task>) — Describes how to do something. Contains prerequisites, numbered steps with commands, expected results, and post-requisites.
Reference (<reference>) — Provides lookup information. Contains structured tables, property lists, and syntax descriptions.

section.p: This typing is what makes DITA content intelligent — a chatbot can identify whether a chunk is conceptual background, a step-by-step procedure, or a parameter reference, and deliver the right type for each user query.

section.title: Why use DITA over unstructured formats?

ul.li: Write once, publish everywhere: Generate HTML5, PDF, EPUB, and custom formats from a single source
Content reuse: Share content across products, audiences, and deliverables using conref and keyref
Consistency: Enforce structure and controlled vocabulary through the DITA schema
AI-driven delivery: Semantic metadata enables chatbot integration, personalized search, and intent-based routing
Scale: Manage documentation from 20 topics to 20,000 topics with the same architecture
Translation efficiency: Typed, modular content integrates smoothly with translation management systems, and reused content is translated only once

section.title: How does DITA compare to unstructured authoring?
section.p: In unstructured authoring (Word, wiki, flat XML/HTML), the content has no enforced schema, no embedded metadata, and no separation between content and formatting. This leads to:

ul.li: Copy-paste duplication instead of true reuse
Inconsistent structure that AI cannot reliably parse
Manual effort to produce each output format
No machine-readable signal about content type or audience

section.p: DITA addresses each of these problems through its type system, reuse mechanisms, and metadata architecture. The samples/before and samples/after files in this directory illustrate these differences concretely.
